5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Site speed is one of those things you know matters but keep putting off. We were the same. We knew the term Core Web Vitals, and we knew our scores weren’t great. But we had no idea where to start, so we just left it alone.
At some point we decided to face it properly. What we learned: trying to fix all three metrics at once is exactly what keeps you stuck. The moment we narrowed it down to “the single slowest one,” both the scores and the work finally started moving. Here’s how it went, including what the numbers actually mean.

First, what the three Core Web Vitals numbers mean
Core Web Vitals are the three metrics Google uses to measure whether the user experience is good or bad. Let’s sort them out in plain language first.

• LCP (loading speed): how long it takes for the main content to finish showing. Aim for under 2.5 seconds.

• INP (response to input): how quickly the screen reacts after you tap a button or link. Aim for under 200 milliseconds.

• CLS (layout shift): how much the layout jumps around while loading, moving the spot you were about to tap. Aim for under 0.1.
The key thing is that this is judged by whether three out of four real visitors meet these targets. In other words, loading fast once on your own phone doesn’t mean you pass. What counts is the experience of real users arriving on all kinds of connections and devices.

Where we got stuck: trying to fix all three at once
At first, we tried to deal with all three numbers at the same time.
Lighten the images for LCP, rework the scripts for INP, lock down the ad slot height for CLS — we spread ourselves across everything, and every fix ended up half-finished. There was too much to do, and we couldn’t set priorities.
The problem here wasn’t technical. The cause was diving into the work before figuring out which part was hurting the experience most.

What worked: focus on just the single slowest one
So we changed our thinking. Instead of raising all three evenly, we focused on the single worst one.
In our case, the thing dragging us down was CLS — the layout jumping around while the page loaded. The cause was images and banners loading in late and pushing the already-displayed text downward. Just reserving space for their height up front improved the number dramatically.

• Measure first, and identify the single worst one of the three

• Fix only that one, narrowing in on its cause

• Once fixed, measure again and confirm the effect

• When it settles, move to the next worst one
Once we worked through it one at a time like this, the improvements that had been stuck started flowing. Not taking on everything at once turned out to be the fastest route.

How to measure: look at real-user numbers, not how it feels on your own phone
To find the single slowest one, you have to measure correctly first. A lot of people get stuck here, so here’s our approach.
The important thing is to separate the lab numbers from the real-user numbers. A number measured once in your own environment (a lab value) is handy for tracking down causes, but you can’t judge a pass on it alone. What actually counts toward the score is the experience of the people who really visited your site (the field values).
Roughly, we use them like this:

• First, use real-user numbers to get a rough sense of which of the three is bad

• Then use lab measurements to pinpoint which element on the page is the cause

• After fixing, watch for a few weeks until the real-user numbers improve
The one to watch out for is step 3, the waiting. Field values build up from the data of people who actually visited, so they don’t flip to a perfect score the day after you fix something. Not panicking and tinkering elsewhere when the numbers don’t move right away was the trick to avoiding detours.

An honest limit: speed is the entrance, not everything
There’s one thing we want to be honest about. It’s not as simple as “fix your speed and results will follow.”
Even if it gets faster, if the content doesn’t land, it won’t lead to sign-ups. Even with a perfect score, it means nothing if the path that matters is hard to follow. We see site speed as setting up the entrance so the people who come don’t slip away — not as the move that creates results itself.
That’s exactly why it’s important not to chase the score numbers too hard. We try to look at where actual visitors are dropping off alongside the access data.

To keep it up in operations: don’t call it done after one fix
The biggest waste in speed work is fixing it once, feeling satisfied, and letting it slowly get slow again.
Images pile up, features get added, and before you know it you’re back where you started. To prevent this, you need a setup that treats improvement not as a one-time event but as something you review regularly as part of operations. We keep a record of what we fixed and the date, so we can trace who touched what and when. We gather access numbers in their usual place through the Google Analytics integration, so when something feels slower we can check right away.
Measure, fix the single worst one, record it, measure again. Keeping up this quiet loop is, we feel, the most reliable way to keep a site fast.

MONJI+, a WebOps platform born from the field
MONJI+ is a WebOps platform created to solve the challenges we’ve felt firsthand in the field. MONJI+ brings the “people” involved in website operations together into one “team,” working across every phase of website operations to solve problems.
We’re not aiming to build a finished product from the start. The challenges that truly matter live in the field of website operations, which is why we keep facing the real voices from the field. Taking in each one, we stack up updates that resolve small points of friction, and new features have grown out of the field’s challenges too.

Summary

• Core Web Vitals come down to three: LCP (load within 2.5s), INP (input response within 200ms), and CLS (layout shift within 0.1), judged by whether 75% of real users meet them.

• Trying to fix all three at once leaves you unable to prioritize and stuck. Narrow to the single slowest one and go one at a time — fix, measure, next — and it’s faster.

• Site speed sets up the entrance so visitors don’t slip away; it doesn’t create results on its own. Don’t just chase the score — look at actual drop-off in the data too.

• The biggest waste is calling it done after one fix. Record what you improved and review it regularly, and it’s easier to stay fast.
